title: Streams and conversations carry a generic metadata slot; capabilities is one well-known key inside it
content: Streams and conversations each carry a single metadata slot — a JSON object owned by AMS at the protocol level. AMS owns the slot and the broadcast guarantee; AMS does not own the contents. One key, "capabilities", is well-known by convention as the slot for a stream's declared capability manifest. All other keys are annotations — free-form fields like role, display name, version, contact, homepage. Stream metadata is mutable by the owning account via set_metadata frames; conversation metadata is set at mint and immutable in v1. AMS broadcasts metadata on join (rides on stream_joined) and on every change (stream_metadata frame). The schema of capabilities itself stays application-defined — AMS never validates or constrains its shape.

title: Capability negotiation lives in the agents, not in the protocol
content: The conversation has no canonical capability set. Each stream declares its own capabilities in its metadata; peers read peer metadata and converge on a working agreement themselves. Different streams in the same conversation may negotiate entirely different effective contracts depending on use case — the protocol does not impose a single conversation-wide contract. Re-negotiation is just a metadata write: emit a new metadata object, every subscriber is notified via stream_metadata, peers re-converge. No new primitive, no extra round-trip, no central authority.

title: A previously-deferred sister-spec collapsed into a small wire extension once the right slot was named
content: Capability negotiation was originally listed as a deferred sister-spec — something that would need its own protocol layer to be designed before AMS could be practically deployed across unfamiliar peers. The metadata slot decision collapses that gap. By naming a generic, application-defined metadata field on the existing primitives and broadcasting changes as first-class wire events, AMS gains a minimum-viable capability declaration and re-negotiation surface without growing a new protocol or violating vodka. The slot is generic, the well-known key is one named convention, the schema lives above. The lesson: when a deferred concern can be reduced to "a generic carrier and a broadcast guarantee," it likely belongs in the protocol after all.

title: Metadata payloads are opaque to AMS; conformant implementations must not modify, schema-validate, or filter them
content: A conformant AMS implementation must broadcast metadata changes faithfully and must never modify, schema-validate, or filter the contents of metadata in transit. Stream metadata may only be set by the account that owns the stream; conversation metadata is set at mint by the conversation creator and is immutable in v1. These are wire-level invariants that protect the vodka contract: AMS owns the carrier; agents own the meaning.

title: stream_metadata, set_metadata, annotations, capabilities (well-known key)
content: stream_metadata is the server-broadcast frame emitted on every metadata change for any stream in the conversation. set_metadata is the client-emitted frame that replaces the client's stream metadata in full (not a patch). Annotations are any metadata keys other than the well-known capabilities key — free-form, application-defined, used to introduce a stream to the room. capabilities is the one well-known metadata key reserved by convention for declared capability manifests; its value's schema is application-defined and AMS does not validate it.
